aids.title: Amortized Cost Analogy
aids.content: Think of a **savings account**. You deposit $1 each time you add an element. When resize happens, you 'spend' from savings. After n inserts, you spent < 2n, so each operation 'cost' < $2 on average.

aids.title: Why Double the Capacity?
aids.content:
**Doubling** gives the best balance:
- Total copies for n items < n
- At most 50% wasted space
- Simple to implement

Smaller factors = more copies, larger = more waste

aids.title: Dynamic Array Names
aids.content: **VAL-P**: **V**ector (C++), **A**rrayList (Java), **L**ist (Python), **P**HP array. All are dynamic arrays with different names!

aids.title: 1/4 Shrink Rule
aids.content: Shrink at **1/4** capacity, not 1/2. Why? At 1/2, one add + one remove = grow + shrink forever! 1/4 provides a **buffer zone** to prevent this thrashing.

aids.title: Dynamic Array Trade-off
aids.content: **Flexibility costs space**: Dynamic arrays trade some memory (capacity > size) for the ability to resize. Static arrays waste nothing but can't grow.

aids.title: O(1) vs O(n) Operations
aids.content: **END is your FRIEND**: Operations at the END are O(1). Operations in the MIDDLE or BEGINNING are O(n) because of shifting.
aids.forRemembering: Time complexity of dynamic array operations
